methods.description: Phytoplankton samples were taken from lakes situated mostly in and near the low altitude areas of the Eastern Alps near human settlements, along an altitudinal gradient between the Eastern lowlands, the extreme eastern slopes of the Eastern Alps in Austria (< 300 m), and a few lakes sampled in low and mid altitudes of the Central Eastern Alps (highest altitude 1600 m).

steps.description: Phytoplankton samples (mostly plankton 20 µm mesh size net-hauls and surface water samples) were taken from lakes along an altitudinal gradient between the Eastern lowlands, the extreme eastern slopes of the Eastern Alps in Austria (< 300 m), and a few lakes sampled in low and mid altitudes of the Central Eastern Alps (highest altitude 1600 m).
steps.searchable_summary: collect phytoplankton samples from lakes using plankton net hauls

steps.description: Phytoplankton samples (mostly plankton 20 µm mesh size net-hauls and surface water samples) were taken from lakes along an altitudinal gradient between the Eastern lowlands, the extreme eastern slopes of the Eastern Alps in Austria (< 300 m), and a few lakes sampled in low and mid altitudes of the Central Eastern Alps (highest altitude 1600 m).
steps.searchable_summary: collect phytoplankton samples from lakes using surface water collection

methods.description: Preparation of sediment and dry mount samples for Light Microscopy (LM) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM).

steps.description: Sediment samples were treated with H 2 O 2
steps.searchable_summary: treat sediment samples with hydrogen peroxide

steps.description: carbonate rich samples pre-treated with HCl (hydrochloric acid).
steps.searchable_summary: pre-treat carbonate rich samples with hydrochloric acid

steps.description: In several cases, dry mounts were also made (especially for Urosolenia).
steps.searchable_summary: create dry mounts from samples

steps.description: After washing several times, the materials were embedded into Naphrax (for LM) or dried on cleaned cover glass pieces glued to aluminium stubs, coated with gold-palladium using a Balzers sputter coater SCD 040 and studied with a Philipps XL20 SEM.
steps.searchable_summary: wash sample materials multiple times

steps.description: After washing several times, the materials were embedded into Naphrax (for LM)
steps.searchable_summary: embed washed sample materials into Naphrax for light microscopy

steps.description: or dried on cleaned cover glass pieces glued to aluminium stubs, coated with gold-palladium using a Balzers sputter coater SCD 040 and studied with a Philipps XL20 SEM.
steps.searchable_summary: dry washed sample materials on cover glass pieces glued to aluminium stubs for SEM

steps.description: coated with gold-palladium using a Balzers sputter coater SCD 040 and studied with a Philipps XL20 SEM.
steps.searchable_summary: coat dried samples on stubs with gold-palladium using a sputter coater for SEM

methods.description: Studying prepared samples using Light Microscopy (LM) and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and capturing images for analysis.

steps.description: studied with a Philipps XL20 SEM.
steps.searchable_summary: study prepared stubs using scanning electron microscopy

steps.description: We used bright field and ICT light microscopy with up to 1000-times magnifications (Nikon BMX50)
steps.searchable_summary: study prepared slides using bright field and ICT light microscopy

steps.description: with a Progress camera for the BW photographs.
steps.searchable_summary: capture black and white photographs during light microscopy

steps.description: Scale bars are included on every photograph
steps.searchable_summary: include scale bars on microscopy photographs

steps.description: and described in each figure legend.
steps.searchable_summary: describe scale bars in figure legends
outputs: figure legends with scale bar descriptions

methods.description: Identifying diatom taxa and measuring their dimensions based on microscopy images and reference literature/databases.

steps.description: The taxonomic part refers to the classification system by Round, Crawford and Mann (1990)
steps.searchable_summary: refer to Round, Crawford and Mann (1990) classification system for taxonomy
inputs: Round, Crawford and Mann (1990) classification system

steps.description: taxa identifications by Krammer and Lange-Bertalot (1991) supplemented by the recent volumes by Houk et al. (2010Houk et al. ( , 2014Houk et al. ( and 2017.
steps.searchable_summary: use identification keys by Krammer and Lange-Bertalot and Houk et al. for taxa identification

steps.description: Internet information from Diatoms of North America (www.diatoms.org), reference papers for Lindavia (Nakov et al. 2015) and Pantocsekiella (Ács et al. 2016), and taxa descriptions from specific literature (Stachura-Suchoples and Williams 2009 for Contricribra) were also used.
steps.searchable_summary: use online databases and specific literature for taxa identification
inputs: Diatoms of North America online database
specific reference papers (Nakov et al. 2015, Ács et al. 2016, Stachura-Suchoples and Williams 2009)

steps.description: Valve and other dimensions (diameter, striae number etc.) were based on LM and SEM images using a calibration scale for every image.
steps.searchable_summary: measure diatom valve and other dimensions from LM and SEM images using a calibration scale

steps.description: These data are compared to the most recent literature, especially the studies by Houk et al. (2010Houk et al. ( , 2014Houk et al. ( and 2017.
steps.searchable_summary: compare measured dimensions to recent literature

methods.method_name: Environmental Data Assessment and Trophic Classification
methods.description: Assessment of environmental data (conductivity, temperature) and determination of trophic status based on various criteria and standards.

steps.description: The species findings are supplemented with simultaneously assessed environmental data (conductivity and temperature)
steps.searchable_summary: assess environmental data (conductivity and temperature) simultaneously with sampling

steps.description: and general ecological and water quality criteria from our earlier studies (Rott 1984;Pipp & Rott 1995) related to a continuous lake survey program adjusted to meet the requirements of the European Water Framework Directive (European Union, 2000) for the large lakes and additional annual surveys of bathing lakes by the regional governments (Oberösterreichische Landesregierung, 2018).
steps.searchable_summary: use general ecological and water quality criteria from earlier studies and lake survey programs

steps.description: The trophic classifications are mainly based on biological and water chemical components (nitrate, total phosphorus, chlorophyll, phytoplankton composition and biovolume, according to OECD standards (Salmaso et al. 2006;Wolfram et al. 2013Wolfram et al. , 2014Wolfram et al. , 2015Dokulil 2017).
steps.searchable_summary: base trophic classifications on biological and water chemical components according to OECD standards
inputs: water chemical components data (nitrate, total phosphorus, chlorophyll)
OECD standards (Salmaso et al. 2006; Wolfram et al. 2013-2015; Dokulil 2017)

steps.description: The trophic gradient was simulated using the numerical transformation of the status classes as follows: 1 (oligotrophic), 2 (oligo-mesotrophic), 5 (mesotrophic) and 8 (eutrophic) (see Figs 29 and 30, Table S2).
steps.searchable_summary: numerically transform trophic status classes to simulate a trophic gradient

methods.description: Performing Canonical correspondence analysis (CCA) on diatom presence/absence data to classify samples and taxa in relation to site characteristics.

steps.description: The constrained ordination of the diatom samples (presence/absence data) is based on CCA (Canonical correspondence analysis)
steps.searchable_summary: prepare diatom sample data as presence/absence matrix

steps.description: to classify samples and taxa in relation to site characteristics (lake size, altitude, lake type, trophic status and conductivity).
steps.searchable_summary: prepare site characteristics data for analysis

steps.description: Lake types were operated as nominal variables: L (large and deep perialpine and alpine lakes), G (gravel pit lakes, deep), P (shallow ponds), D (small deep lakes) and R (reservoirs, riverine lakes).
steps.searchable_summary: define and encode lake types as nominal variables
inputs: lake type definitions (L, G, P, D, R)

steps.description: CCA was done in CANOCO 5 (Šmilauer & Lepš 2014).
steps.searchable_summary: perform Canonical correspondence analysis (CCA) using CANOCO software

steps.description: The significance of the environmental variables for species distributions used in CCA was statistically tested by the performance of Monte Carlo-Permutation tests (1999 permutations, unrestricted).
steps.searchable_summary: statistically test the significance of environmental variables in CCA using permutation tests
inputs: Monte Carlo-Permutation test parameters (1999 permutations, unrestricted)
outputs: statistical significance values for environmental variables
